title: Sortable invoice list with column-context summary footer and hover-row totals
design_brief: You're building the invoice list view for a B2B accounting tool (think QuickBooks, Pilot, Anrok). Bookkeepers scan dozens of invoices and need totals at the bottom that respect current filters. Pain point: most lists show row data but lose aggregate context; switching filters means the user mentally re-totals. The bar: Stripe's billing dashboard, QuickBooks Online's invoice view, Pilot's books page.
anti_patterns_to_avoid: Sticky total at top that never matches the visible row count
Sum row labeled 'Total' without filter context
Numbers displayed without right-alignment or tabular figures
Currency without consistent decimal column alignment
'Show 100 per page' pagination instead of virtualization
Generic 'Sort by' dropdown instead of clickable column headers
ux_reasoning:
An invoice list has TWO numerical jobs: per-row data (each invoice) and aggregate (sum, count, avg) for what's currently filtered. Most lists do one and forget the other.

Layout: standard table with header row, scrolling body, and a STICKY footer that recomputes aggregates from currently-visible (filtered) rows. The footer is full-width-divider visible. Aggregates: count of invoices, sum of amount, average days outstanding. Each aggregate aligns under its respective column.

Filter changes: when a filter is applied (e.g., 'unpaid only'), the footer immediately recomputes. A subtle 200ms tween animates the aggregate numbers via digit-roll (only changed digits animate). The footer is the LIVE aggregate view of the user's current query.

Row hover: hovering a row not only highlights it but ALSO shows a small floating sub-total beside the cursor (or sticky in the footer) that says 'rows above selected: $X · this row: $Y'. This is the Notion-database move applied to invoices. Useful for 'what's the total of these 3 things'.

Multi-select with Shift+click: select a range of rows; the footer aggregates show ONLY for selected rows (with a small indicator 'selected: 12 · $24,520'). Click again to deselect.

Columns: stable for accounting — Invoice # (tabular), Customer (truncate with hover), Date (relative — '2d ago' or absolute via toggle), Amount (currency, decimal-aligned), Status (chip).

Motion: digit-roll for aggregates on filter change. Row hover instant. NO transitions on sort (instant reorder).

Rejected: AI-generated summaries below the footer ('Most invoices are from Acme this month'). Adds chrome; expert users want raw numbers. Also rejected: a chart at the top showing trends. Different view, different page.

design_tokens.colors: Footer bg: oklch(0.97 0.005 250) (subtle distinction from rows). Footer text: oklch(0.18 0.005 250). Status chips: standard semantic (paid green, overdue red, draft gray). Row hover bg: oklch(0.97 0.005 250). Multi-select bg: oklch(0.96 0.04 250).
design_tokens.typography: All numbers: 'JetBrains Mono' tabular-nums for decimal alignment. Body: Inter 13px regular. Footer aggregates: 14px medium tabular-nums. Status chip: 11px medium uppercase tracking-wider.
design_tokens.spacing: Row: 36px tall. Column gap: 16px. Footer: 48px tall, sticky bottom.
design_tokens.motion: Digit-roll on aggregate change: 200ms cubic-bezier(0.22, 1, 0.36, 1). Row hover instant. Sort change instant.

component_architecture: <InvoiceList<TInvoice> invoices filters onFilterChange>. Generic over invoice shape. <FilterBar /> for filter pills (re-uses primitive from batch 02). <Footer aggregates={...} /> with digit-roll on changes. <RowAggregates hoveredRow selectedRows /> floats next to cursor showing partial-totals. useDigitRoll() hook from batch 02.
interaction_details: Apply filter pill: row list reflows; footer aggregates recompute with 200ms digit-roll on changed digits.
Shift+click rows: builds a multi-selection; footer aggregates switch to 'selected' mode with N rows and updated sum/avg.
Hover row: bg shift + small partial-total floats beside cursor showing 'sum above + this row'.
Decimal-aligned currency: $ symbol left, integer right-aligned, fraction left-aligned at decimal point.
Footer is sticky at bottom; stays visible during long-table scroll.
accessibility_considerations: Footer uses role='status' aria-live='polite' so screen readers hear aggregate changes (debounced via the digit-roll). Status chips have aria-label including status name (not color alone). Multi-select state announced. Keyboard: Tab to header, Space sorts, Shift+arrow extends selection. Reduced-motion: digit-roll becomes instant.
what_makes_this_distinctive: 1) Footer aggregates RESPECT THE FILTER — when you filter to 'overdue', the total shows overdue total. Selection-aware aggregates further refine. 2) Decimal-aligned currency via integer/fraction split — numbers stay perfectly aligned even at varying magnitudes. 3) Digit-roll on aggregate updates — eye sees the change without re-reading the whole row.

title: Markdown WYSIWYG with arrow-key block-handle reveal and inline raw-mode toggle
design_brief: You're building a markdown-aware editor (think Bear, iA Writer's hybrid mode, Obsidian Live Preview). Pain point: most markdown editors are either pure raw-text (forcing users to read syntax) or pure WYSIWYG (losing the ability to edit syntax precisely). The bar: Bear's hybrid view, Obsidian Live Preview, iA Writer.
anti_patterns_to_avoid: Syntax that disappears completely when cursor isn't on it
ux_reasoning:
The genius of Live Preview: when the cursor is INSIDE a block, the raw markdown is visible (so you can edit syntax precisely). When the cursor leaves, that block renders. The user gets both worlds without modes.

Mechanics: cursor enters a heading line — block becomes raw (`## Heading` shown). Cursor leaves — block renders as a styled heading. Same for bold, italic, code spans, links, images, lists. The TRANSITION happens at cursor crossing, not at edit completion.

Block handle: when a block is focused (or hovered), a small 6-dot handle appears 24px to the left. Pressing Tab on the handle opens a contextual menu (duplicate, delete, transform). Same as Notion. Critically, the handle is ALWAYS present when the block is focused (not hover-only) — keyboard users get it.

Inline raw-mode toggle: pressing Cmd+Shift+M toggles the entire document between WYSIWYG mode and raw mode. Useful for users who want to see/edit all syntax at once.

Block arrows: Cmd+Shift+Up/Down moves the current block. Same as the Notion-style editor from batch 02, but here ALSO works for selected text within a block to reorder list items.

Links: in WYSIWYG, links show as styled text. Click a link with Cmd held: opens it. Click without Cmd: cursor moves inside the link AND the raw markdown is revealed (`[text](url)`). Edit the URL or label, move out, renders again.

Images: render inline in WYSIWYG. Cursor on image line: reveals `![alt](url)`. Click image: opens lightbox.

Motion: cursor-cross syntax transition is INSTANT (no fade — would feel laggy on rapid arrow-key nav). Block move via Cmd+Shift+arrow: 120ms FLIP.

Rejected: a side-by-side preview pane. iA Writer pioneered the hybrid approach exactly because side-by-side doubles the screen and forces eye-tracking. Hybrid mode is the right answer for prose-heavy workflows. Also rejected: AI rephrasing buttons. Out of scope for the editor primitive.

design_tokens.colors: Body: oklch(0.18 0.005 60). H1: oklch(0.18 0.005 60) (same as body — only size matters). Code span bg: oklch(0.95 0.005 60). Link: oklch(0.55 0.18 250). Block handle: oklch(0.6 0.005 60) at 60% opacity.
design_tokens.typography: Body: 'Tiempos Text', 'Newsreader', Georgia, serif 17px / 28px (book-like). Headings: same serif at 28/22/18px. Code: 'JetBrains Mono' 15px on subtle bg.
design_tokens.spacing: Block margin: 0 (paragraphs flow). Block handle: 24px left of block at 6px from line height start. Code span padding: 2px horizontal.
design_tokens.motion: Cursor-cross syntax reveal: instant. Block move FLIP: 120ms cubic-bezier(0.22, 1, 0.36, 1). NO fade transitions on text content.

component_architecture: <MarkdownEditor /> with a contentEditable area. Block tracking via parsed AST. <Block ast={...} cursorInside={...} /> renders raw when cursorInside, rendered otherwise. useCursorPosition() tracks selection and computes which block contains the cursor. useBlockHandle() reveals handle on focus.
interaction_details: Cursor enters a heading line: raw markdown `## Heading` appears for that block. Edit syntax precisely.
Cursor leaves block: raw markdown re-renders as styled heading instantly.
Cmd+Shift+M: toggles entire document to/from raw mode for full-syntax editing.
Cmd+Shift+Up/Down: moves the current block. Works on any block kind.
Tab on block handle (when focused): opens contextual menu (duplicate, transform, delete).
accessibility_considerations: Each block is real semantic HTML (<h1>, <ul>, <blockquote>, <pre>) — screen readers traverse natively. Block handles are real buttons. Reduced-motion: block-move FLIP becomes instant. Cursor-cross transition is already instant. Color is supplementary.
what_makes_this_distinctive: 1) Cursor-cross raw/rendered transition — see syntax while editing, rendered when cursor leaves. iA Writer's hybrid mode, faithfully implemented. 2) Cmd+Shift+M toggle for full-raw mode when needed (e.g., complex syntax fixes). 3) Same semantic HTML output regardless of mode — screen readers and downstream consumers see consistent structure.

title: Multiplayer document outline with collapsed-section sync and per-user reading position
design_brief: You're building the document outline navigation for a long-form collaborative doc (think Notion's outline, Coda's table of contents, Google Docs document outline). Pain point: most outlines don't show where collaborators are reading; collapsing sections doesn't sync. The bar: Notion's outline + presence, Coda's TOC, Pitch's slide thumbnails.
anti_patterns_to_avoid: Outline that's a hidden sidebar requiring click to reveal
Collapse state that's per-user only (each user reorganizing the outline differently)
Outline that doesn't update as sections are added/removed
Click outline item that scrolls jankily
Empty outline shown for short docs
ux_reasoning:
For long docs (specs, design briefs, RFC drafts), the outline is the map. With collaborators, the map should show WHERE everyone is reading so the discussion can be coordinated ('let's all jump to section 3').

Layout: outline as a 240px right rail (collapsible) showing heading hierarchy. Each heading is clickable to scroll to that section.

Reading position dots: next to each heading, small colored dots show which collaborators are currently reading that section. Determined by viewport center being within the section's vertical span. Updates as collaborators scroll. Click a dot to 'follow user' (your scroll locks to theirs).

Collapsed sections: clicking the chevron next to a heading collapses that section's children in the outline (visual collapse). This is a PERSONAL view, not synced — collapse preferences are per-user.

New sections: when a collaborator adds a heading, it appears in the outline with a subtle 200ms slide-in. After 5s, it's just part of the outline. Removed: fades out 200ms.

Follow-user mode: clicking a user dot enters Follow mode for that user; the rail header shows 'Following Alice'. Your scroll lerps to match theirs. Esc exits.

Position history: a small 'breadcrumb' at the top shows the last 5 sections YOU visited (in tabs). Click any to jump back. This is the killer feature: long-doc navigation has memory.

Motion: dot movements (collaborator scrolls): 200ms ease-out top position. Click outline → scroll: smooth 400ms. Follow-user lock: 250ms lerp to target.

Rejected: showing collaborator names ALL the time. Dots only; hover for name. Keeps outline scannable. Also rejected: AI-generated section summaries. Out of scope.

design_tokens.colors: Outline bg: oklch(0.97 0.005 250) or transparent. Current section: oklch(0.95 0.04 250) bg + 2px left border in oklch(0.55 0.18 250). Dots: standard 12-user palette. Following indicator: oklch(0.95 0.08 60) bg.
design_tokens.typography: Heading items: Inter 13px regular; current section 13px medium. Indented children: 12px. Breadcrumb tabs: 12px regular.
design_tokens.spacing: Indent per level: 12px. Dot: 6px circle with -space-x-1 stacking for multiple. Rail width: 240px collapsed to 32px (icon only).
design_tokens.motion: Dot movement: 200ms ease-out top transition. Outline-click scroll: 400ms cubic-bezier(0.22, 1, 0.36, 1). Follow-user lerp: 250ms ease-out.

component_architecture: <DocOutline doc collaborators currentUserId followingId> renders the right rail. <OutlineItem heading children dots /> renders one heading line with presence dots. useReadingPosition(scrollY, sections) computes which section each user is in. useFollowUser() locks scroll to another user's.
interaction_details: Outline rail shows hierarchy. Current section (where YOUR cursor is) has 2px left border + bg highlight.
Collaborators reading each section appear as colored dots next to that heading. Hover for name; click for Follow mode.
Following mode: rail shows 'Following Alice' banner; your scroll lerps to her position. Esc exits.
Recent visited breadcrumb (top of rail) shows last 5 sections you've been in — click any to jump back.
Outline updates as collaborators add/remove headings (200ms slide-in/out).
accessibility_considerations: aside has aria-label. Heading items are real buttons. Dots have aria-label='Follow {user}'. Following banner uses role='status' aria-live='polite'. Reduced-motion: dot transitions become instant.
what_makes_this_distinctive: 1) Reading-position dots show WHERE collaborators are in long docs — not just 'who's here'. 2) Click dot to enter Follow mode — scroll locks to that user. 3) Recent-visited breadcrumb at rail top — long-doc navigation has memory.

title: Drag-to-reorder list with magnetic snap and self-revealing drop zones
design_brief: You're building drag-to-reorder for a task list (think Things 3, OmniFocus, Apple Reminders). Pain point: most drag implementations have weak feedback — the drop target is unclear until release, and snapping feels imprecise. The bar: Things 3 (the gold standard for kinetic feedback), Apple Reminders, Notion's drag handles.
anti_patterns_to_avoid: Drag preview that's a low-opacity ghost of the original row
Drop indicator that's a 1px line — too subtle
Auto-scroll near viewport edges that's too aggressive
Drop preview that snaps only at release
Grab cursor that's the same as the row cursor
ux_reasoning:
Reordering should feel physical: when you lift an item, the world makes room; when you hover a position, that gap WANTS you to drop. Magnetic snap with anticipatory motion.

Mechanics: pressing pointerdown on the drag handle (or 200ms long-press on the row) lifts the item — it elevates with a subtle shadow + scale 1.02 + cursor becomes grabbing. As you drag, OTHER ROWS ANIMATE to make room: rows above the cursor stay; rows below the cursor shift down by row-height with 200ms ease-out. The 'drop zone' is the implicit gap created.

Magnetic snap: the dragged item snaps to the nearest gap when within 12px. Visual feedback: dragged item's shadow intensifies. This is Things 3's exact behavior — the gap pulls you in.

Drop release: dragged item animates from cursor position to final gap (200ms cubic-bezier(0.22, 1, 0.36, 1)). Scale back to 1.0, shadow dissolves. Light haptic on commit.

Auto-scroll: when dragging near top/bottom 80px of viewport, scroll at speed proportional to proximity (5-15px per frame). Smooth, never jerky. Stop instantly when cursor moves away from edge.

Cancel: pressing Esc during drag cancels — item returns to original position with 250ms ease-out. Other rows return to their original positions.

Motion: row reflow during drag: 200ms ease-out PER row (staggered slightly so the wave reads). Drop snap: 200ms cubic-bezier(0.22, 1, 0.36, 1). Cancel return: 250ms ease-out.

Rejected: a 'drop here' separator line. Magnetic gap is stronger feedback. Also rejected: drag preview that's a screenshot. Real item + shadow is more honest.

design_tokens.colors: Lifted item shadow: 0 8px 24px oklch(0.18 0.005 250 / 0.15). Drop-zone gap bg: oklch(0.97 0.005 250) (subtle warmth). Drag handle: oklch(0.55 0.005 250) on hover, oklch(0.85 0.005 250) idle.
design_tokens.typography: List items: Inter 14px regular.
design_tokens.spacing: Row: 44px tall. Drag handle: 16px wide, 24px from left edge. Lift elevation: -2px translateY for shadow visibility.
design_tokens.motion: Row reflow during drag: 200ms ease-out. Drop snap: 200ms cubic-bezier(0.22, 1, 0.36, 1). Cancel return: 250ms ease-out. Auto-scroll: 60Hz step.

component_architecture: <SortableList<TItem extends BaseItem> items onReorder>. Generic over items. Internally uses Pointer Events directly (not react-dnd or dnd-kit — both are great but heavy for a sortable list). useDragReorder() hook handles state machine: idle → lifting → dragging → dropping. Row positions computed from indices + drag offset.
interaction_details: Pointerdown on handle: row lifts (shadow + 1.02 scale). As you drag, other rows reflow to make room over 200ms ease-out.
Drop near a row gap: magnetic snap brings dragged item to gap.
Release: dragged item animates to final gap over 200ms. Single 8ms haptic.
Esc during drag: cancels, item returns to original position over 250ms.
Auto-scroll within 80px of viewport edge during drag, proportional to proximity.
accessibility_considerations: Drag handle is a real button with aria-label='Drag to reorder'. Keyboard alternative: focused row + Cmd+Shift+arrow moves it. SortableList announces 'Item moved to position N' via aria-live. Reduced-motion: row reflows become instant; drop animation becomes instant. Color is supplementary.
what_makes_this_distinctive: 1) Other rows REFLOW as you drag to create a real gap — no separator line needed, the gap IS the drop zone. 2) Magnetic snap when within 12px of a gap — feels physical, not pixel-precise. 3) Single haptic ONLY on commit (not on drag start or hover) — minimal, respect for the gesture.

title: Diff visualization of two time-series with synchronized brush and delta lane
design_brief: You're building before/after metric comparison for a release-impact tool (think Datadog's deploy compare, Honeycomb's bubble-up, Sentry's release health). Pain point: two charts side-by-side force eye-jumps; overlay charts hide which series is which. The bar: Datadog's deploy markers, Vercel Observability comparison.
anti_patterns_to_avoid: Side-by-side identical charts with no shared axis
Stacked area chart of A and B (loses individual shapes)
Tooltip showing both values without delta
Color confusion when both lines are similar hues
Generic 'compare' toggle that just adds a second line
ux_reasoning:
Comparison should be FIRST-CLASS, not an overlay afterthought. Three lanes stacked: (1) Series A line, (2) Series B line, (3) DELTA between them as a filled area (positive = green above zero, negative = red below). Shared X axis, synchronized brushing. The delta lane is the headline — it tells the story.

Brushing on any lane brushes all three. Hovering shows aligned crosshair across all three with values + delta in a single legend. Delta sign-flips are annotated automatically ('became positive at 14:02').

Motion: lane transitions on brush are 250ms FLIP. Sign-flip annotations fade in at first appearance only, then stay.

Rejected: bar-chart deltas — line/area form-shape is more readable for trends.

design_tokens.colors: Series A: oklch(0.55 0.18 250). Series B: oklch(0.55 0.16 25). Delta positive fill: oklch(0.55 0.14 145 / 0.3). Delta negative fill: oklch(0.6 0.18 25 / 0.3). Zero line: oklch(0.5 0.005 250) 1px dashed.
design_tokens.typography: Axis labels: 11px tabular-nums. Sign-flip annotation: 11px italic.
design_tokens.spacing: Each lane: 120px tall. Gap between lanes: 8px. Shared X axis at bottom: 24px.
design_tokens.motion: Brush sync: instant across lanes. Sign-flip annotation appear: 200ms ease-out opacity.

component_architecture: <DiffChart seriesA seriesB />. Three <Lane /> children sharing a brushContext. Delta computed as `b[i] - a[i]` per timestamp.
interaction_details: Three lanes: A line, B line, A-B delta as filled area. Shared X axis.
Hover anywhere: crosshair across all three; legend shows A, B, delta at that timestamp.
Brush on any lane: all three brush together.
Sign-flip in delta is annotated inline ('became positive at 14:02').
accessibility_considerations: Each lane is a separate <figure> with descriptive aria-label. Underlying data table available to SR users. Reduced-motion: brushes instant.
what_makes_this_distinctive: 1) Delta lane is the headline — area shape directly shows magnitude AND sign of difference. 2) Synchronized brushing across all three lanes — explore time windows once. 3) Sign-flip annotations call out story moments without user querying.

title: Interactive workspace template chooser with live preview as you hover
design_brief: You're building the new-workspace setup for a CMS-like tool (think Notion templates, Airtable templates, Figma file templates). Pain point: most template choosers are static thumbnails — clicking commits, often to disappointment. The bar: Figma's community templates with live preview, Notion's template gallery, Webflow's start-from templates.
anti_patterns_to_avoid: Loading the actual template on hover (slow + wasteful)
'Use template' button that opens a destructive create flow
ux_reasoning:
A template chooser should let users 'try on' templates instantly. Hovering a card should preview it in a large preview pane LIVE (without committing). Only on explicit click does the template clone into a new workspace.

Layout: left rail filter (by use case: Engineering, Marketing, etc.). Center: grid of template cards (180×120 thumbnails). Right pane (50% width): LIVE preview of the hovered/selected template — shows the rendered first page of the template at scaled-down but readable size.

Hover behavior: hovering a card loads its preview into the right pane after 150ms intent delay. Preview is a pre-rendered static screenshot at first (instant), then upgrades to a real interactive embedded version after another 500ms.

Click a card: 'Use this template' bar appears at bottom with template name and 'Use template' button. Clicking creates a new workspace from the template.

No destructive actions on preview — preview is read-only.

Rejected: AI-generated bespoke template per query. Adds AI complexity to a chooser; users want curated quality.

design_tokens.colors: Card hover bg: oklch(0.97 0.005 250). Selected card border: 2px oklch(0.55 0.18 250). Preview pane bg: oklch(0.99 0.005 250). Use bar bg: oklch(0.18 0.005 250) text white.
design_tokens.typography: Template name: Inter 14px medium. Description: 12px regular. Card grid: 4 col on wide, 2 on narrow.
design_tokens.spacing: Card: 180×140 (image + name). Preview pane: 50vw min, 720px max-height. Use bar: 56px tall.
design_tokens.motion: Preview swap: 200ms crossfade. Use bar slide-up: 200ms ease-out.

component_architecture: <TemplateChooser templates onUseTemplate>. <TemplateCard /> dispatches hover/click. <PreviewPane template /> shows static screenshot then upgrades to interactive iframe.
interaction_details: Hover card: static preview screenshot appears in right pane after 150ms. After another 500ms, upgrades to interactive iframe.
Click card: selects it. Use bar appears at bottom with 'Use template' button.
Filter rail on left for use case.
Preview is read-only — never destructive.
accessibility_considerations: Cards are real buttons with aria-pressed. Preview pane has aria-label. iframe has title. Reduced-motion: preview swap instant.
what_makes_this_distinctive: 1) Live preview pane shows real template content on hover (screenshot → iframe upgrade) — try-on without commit. 2) Use bar at bottom is the only commit path — preview is always read-only. 3) Filter rail organizes by use case, not by tag — relevance over taxonomy.

title: Conditional question survey with branch flattening and back-tracking history
design_brief: You're building a multi-branch survey/form (think Typeform, Tally, SurveyMonkey for complex flows). Branching means question 5 might be Q5a OR Q5b depending on Q3 answer. Pain point: most multi-branch forms either lose user's answers on back-navigation or trap them in dead-end paths.
anti_patterns_to_avoid: Browser back button doesn't preserve answers
Modal 'Are you sure you want to go back?'
Progress bar showing 'Step X of Y' that's wrong when branching
Generic next button without keyboard shortcut
No way to skip optional questions
Answers lost if you start over
ux_reasoning:
Branching surveys need three things: visible path so user knows where they are, working back-navigation, and a stable progress signal despite branches.

Path sidebar shows visited questions as breadcrumb chips; not-yet-visited remain hidden. Back navigation preserves all answers; forward re-traverses the existing path or reroutes if an earlier answer was changed.

Progress: instead of 'step X of Y', show 'About 3 questions left' calculated from active path. Updates on branching.

Q&A renders one-at-a-time, full-viewport, large type. Enter advances; Shift+Enter on multi-line. Esc opens path sidebar.

Motion: question transition is 200ms ease-out slide + opacity. Reduced-motion: instant.

design_tokens.colors: Bg: oklch(0.99 0.005 60). Question text: oklch(0.18 0.005 60) 28px. Path chip: oklch(0.96 0.005 250) bg.
design_tokens.typography: Question: serif 28px (Newsreader). Answer input: 18px. Sidebar chip: 12px.
design_tokens.spacing: Question centered with 80px top padding. Sidebar 280px right rail (toggleable).
design_tokens.motion: Question slide: 200ms cubic-bezier(0.22, 1, 0.36, 1) translateY(8px → 0).

component_architecture: <Survey questions={...} branchLogic={...}>. State: { path: questionId[], answers: Map }. branchLogic is fn that returns next question id given answer.
interaction_details: One question per screen, large type. Enter advances. Back button preserves answers.
Progress shows 'About 3 questions left' (computed from active path), not 'X of Y' which lies on branches.
Branching: answer to Q3 determines Q4 (Q4a or Q4b). Back-tracking lets user change answer and re-traverse.
Choice answers shown with letter prefix (A, B, C) — keyboard shortcuts.
accessibility_considerations: Question is h2 with proper semantic order. Inputs labeled. Reduced-motion: question slide disabled. Keyboard: full nav, letter shortcuts for choices.
what_makes_this_distinctive: 1) 'About N questions left' computed from active path — honest about branching, doesn't lie like 'step X of Y'. 2) Back-navigation preserves answers — re-traversal is supported, not punished. 3) One question per screen at large type — focused decision per moment.

title: 3D timeline of events as floating cards on a curve
design_brief: You're building a 3D event-history visualization for a company history page or a project retrospective (think Apple's Today at Apple narratives, NASA mission timelines, Studio Mesh case studies).
anti_patterns_to_avoid: Flat timeline rendered in 3D for no reason
Scroll-driven camera that ignores user input
Cards that always face camera (lose 3D feel)
No way to jump to specific year
ux_reasoning:
3D earns its weight only if events have meaningful ARRANGEMENT in space. We place cards on a 3D curve (helix or sine wave) — each card represents one event. Camera follows a path that brings each card to focus as user scrolls. Cards subtly billboard but keep a slight tilt for depth.

Navigation: scroll advances the camera along curve. Side rail shows year scrubber — drag to jump.

DOF blurs cards not at focal plane (the focused card is always sharp).

design_tokens.colors: Bg: vertical gradient oklch(0.18 0.005 250) → oklch(0.05 0.005 280). Card bg: white with subtle inner glow.
design_tokens.typography: Card title: serif 18px. Year tag: mono 11px tabular.
design_tokens.spacing: Cards: 240×180. Spacing on curve: 60 units per year.
design_tokens.motion: Camera follow: 60Hz lerp 0.08 factor. Year scrubber drag: real-time, snap on release.

component_architecture: <Timeline3D events={...}>. <Canvas> with <CurvedCardPath />, <CameraOnCurve />, <YearScrubber /> (HTML overlay).
interaction_details: Scroll: camera advances along curve. Each card comes into focus as it nears the focal plane.
Year scrubber (right rail): drag to jump to specific year; camera lerps to that card.
DOF blurs out-of-focus cards.
Cards are billboards but tilted for depth feel.
accessibility_considerations: sr-only <ol> of events available. Keyboard: arrow keys advance/retreat card focus. Reduced-motion: camera jumps to cards instead of lerping.
what_makes_this_distinctive: 1) Cards on a real 3D curve (CatmullRom spline) — geographic arrangement of time, not just a list in 3D. 2) DOF binds focus to current card — cinematic. 3) Year scrubber gives precise navigation alongside scroll.

title: Photo editor with single-thumb mobile sliders and desktop keyboard fine-tune
design_brief: You're building a photo editor (think Apple Photos edits, VSCO, Lightroom mobile). On mobile, every adjustment must be one-thumb reachable. On desktop, keyboard arrows fine-tune by 1 unit and Shift+arrow by 10.
anti_patterns_to_avoid: Desktop UI that's just mobile stretched
ux_reasoning:
Mobile: bottom-anchored toolbar with one-tap effect selectors above a single horizontal slider. The slider spans full width with the active value floating above the thumb (large 24pt). Slider is thumb-zone safe.

Desktop: same effects in a right rail, but each effect has a number input + slider. Arrow keys ±1, Shift+arrow ±10. Cmd+Z undoes per-adjustment.

design_tokens.colors: Slider track: oklch(0.85 0.005 250). Filled portion: oklch(0.55 0.18 250). Thumb: white circle.
design_tokens.typography: Value above thumb: mono 24px tabular. Effect label: 11px uppercase tracking-wider.
design_tokens.spacing: Mobile bottom toolbar: 88px tall (effects strip + slider). Desktop right rail: 320px.
design_tokens.motion: Slider thumb follows finger 1:1 on mobile. Value above thumb tweens digit-roll on keyboard adjustments only.

component_architecture: <PhotoEditor image adjustments onChange>. Modality-aware: <MobileSliderBar /> or <DesktopRail />. Adjustments are { exposure, contrast, saturation, ... }.
interaction_details: Mobile: effects strip above slider; slider is single full-width thumb-zone. Value floats above thumb as you drag.
Desktop: right rail with all effects. Arrow keys ±1 unit, Shift+arrow ±10. Cmd+Z undoes last adjustment.
Both: image preview updates real-time via CSS filters or canvas.
accessibility_considerations: Range inputs have aria-label. Arrow keys are native to <input type='range'>. Reduced-motion: no change needed.
what_makes_this_distinctive: 1) Mobile slider value floats LARGE above thumb so it's readable mid-drag — never obscured. 2) Same component, different layout per modality (touch slider strip vs desktop number-input rail). 3) Shift+arrow on desktop = 10x step — power users tune faster than dragging.

title: Live region announcer with priority queue and announcement coalescing
design_brief: You're building an aria-live announcer for an SPA with frequent state changes (think the live announcer in Linear, GitHub, Notion). Pain point: many aria-live regions = announcement collisions; debouncing per region loses urgency context. The bar: WAI-ARIA APG live regions, GitHub's a11y patterns.
anti_patterns_to_avoid: Multiple aria-live regions scattered across components
Announcement that fires mid-typing in an input
Long-form messages that take 30s to read
Status messages that don't differentiate from errors
ux_reasoning:
Two priorities: polite (status, ambient) and assertive (errors, urgent). One container each, global. All messages dispatch through a singleton useAnnounce(); duplicates within 1s are dropped; rapid same-priority messages coalesce ('3 items deleted' instead of three 'item deleted').

Assertive interrupts current speech but is rate-limited to max 1 per 2s — prevents user from being battered by errors during a cascade failure.

design_tokens.colors: sr-only — no visual color.

component_architecture: <AnnouncerProvider /> renders 2 hidden regions. useAnnounce(message, urgency) hook dispatches.
interaction_details: Call useAnnounce('Saved'): polite region updates, screen reader announces during next idle moment.
Call useAnnounce('Error', 'assertive'): assertive region updates, interrupts. Rate-limited to 1 per 2s.
Same message within 1s: deduped (dropped).
Rapid polite messages within 300ms: coalesced into single utterance.
accessibility_considerations: Centralized, deduplicating, rate-limiting — solves the 'noisy aria-live' problem. Tested with VoiceOver, NVDA, JAWS.
what_makes_this_distinctive: 1) Single source-of-truth for live announcements eliminates collision bugs. 2) Coalesce same-msg-multiple-times into 'X (5 times)' rather than 5 utterances. 3) Assertive rate-limit prevents cascade-failure spam.

title: Optimistic mutation with visual 'pending' shimmer and rollback choreography
design_brief: You're building a generic optimistic mutation pattern used across an app (think Linear's status changes, Things 3's check-off, Apple Reminders). Pain point: most optimistic UIs don't tell users when something is still pending; users get confused on rollback.
anti_patterns_to_avoid: No visual distinction between pending and confirmed
ux_reasoning: Optimistic mutation should commit to the visual change instantly but show a subtle 'pending' affordance until server ack. On failure: rollback with a 200ms cross-state animation and inline retry. This is broader than the comment pattern in batch 01 — a primitive any mutation can use.

design_tokens.colors: Pending shimmer: 1px gradient stripe on left edge in user color. Rollback: brief red border 200ms. Confirmed: nothing (state is the confirmation).
design_tokens.typography: Inline retry: 12px regular underline.
design_tokens.spacing: Shimmer stripe: 3px on left edge.
design_tokens.motion: Shimmer: 2s linear gradient travel. Rollback shake: 300ms with translateX keyframes.

component_architecture: <OptimisticItem<T> item onMutate>. children render normally; pending state adds shimmer; failure state adds inline retry.
interaction_details: mutate(newValue): UI updates immediately. Pending shimmer appears.
Success: shimmer disappears silently. State is the confirmation.
Failure: value reverts; brief shake; inline retry link.
Single silent retry handles network blips before surfacing error.
accessibility_considerations: Pending uses aria-busy='true'. Failure uses role='alert'. Reduced-motion: shake disabled.
what_makes_this_distinctive: 1) Subtle pending shimmer (not a spinner, not a banner) — invisible peripherally, perceivable on close look. 2) Failure shows inline retry at the failed item, not in a toast. 3) Hook is generic — any mutation can use it with one line.

title: Swiss International Style admin panel with grid lines and Helvetica
design_brief: You're building an admin panel for an academic CMS, museum back-office, or design history archive (think MoMA's website, the Bauhaus archive UI, vignelli.com). Pain point: 'minimalist admin' usually means flat and characterless. The bar: real Swiss design — Müller-Brockmann, Vignelli, the 1972 NYC Subway map.
ux_reasoning:
Swiss IS is about RIGOROUS grid, asymmetric balance, sans-serif at small sizes, abundant negative space, single accent color (often Pantone red). Each element earns its place by grid alignment.

12-col grid VISIBLE as faint guides (toggleable). Helvetica Neue 11px body, 14px label, 24px page title. ALL CAPS rarely; just left-aligned italic numerals as section numbers. Single red accent used SPARINGLY (active state, primary action only). Everything else: pure black on white.

design_tokens.colors: Bg: #ffffff. Ink: #000000. Accent (Pantone Red): oklch(0.55 0.22 25). Rule: #000000 0.5px.
design_tokens.typography: 'Helvetica Neue', Arial: body 11px / 16px / regular. Label 14px / 18px medium. Title 24px / 28px medium. Section numerals: italic 14px.
design_tokens.spacing: 12-col grid, 16px gutters, 16px baseline. Page padding: 32px.
design_tokens.motion: NONE. Static interface.

component_architecture: <SwissAdmin grid>. <Grid /> renders columns as faint lines (toggle). Rows are flat, 32px tall. Buttons: black bg + white text + 1px border for primary; nothing for secondary.
solution:
// SwissAdmin.tsx export function SwissAdmin({ records, onAction }: { records: ReadonlyArray<{ id: string; title: string; date: string; tag: string }>; onAction: (id: string, kind: 'edit' | 'delete') => void }) { return (
01

Archive

{records.map((r, i) => ( ))}
No. TITLE DATE TAG ACTIONS
{String(i + 1).padStart(3, '0')} {r.title} {r.date} {r.tag}
); }
interaction_details: Pure black on white. Grid alignment is the whole design.
Single red accent reserved for DELETE actions (the rare destructive moment).
Italic tabular numerals for section/row numbers — typographic rhythm.
Borders are 0.5px black at 20-40% opacity — restrained dividers.
No animations. The interface is static and declarative.
accessibility_considerations: Pure semantic HTML. Contrast: pure black on white = AAA. Reduced-motion: no motion to disable. Focus rings: 2px solid black outline at 2px offset.
what_makes_this_distinctive: 1) Visible 12-col grid alignment — every element 'snaps' to the grid; no fudged spacing. 2) Single red accent reserved for destructive actions — red MEANS irreversible. 3) Italic tabular section numerals — typographic detail that distinguishes IS from generic minimalism.

title: Sortable table with frozen first column + sticky header for wide datasets
design_brief: You're building a wide-table view (think Airtable's grid with 30+ columns, Excel-style). Pain point: horizontal scroll loses context — you can't tell which row you're looking at without the row label. The bar: Excel, Airtable, Notion's database grid.
anti_patterns_to_avoid: Hidden columns instead of horizontal scroll
Sticky header that overlaps when zoomed
ux_reasoning: Wide tables need both: frozen LABEL column (left) AND sticky HEADER row (top). Both stay visible during scroll in either direction. CSS position: sticky handles this elegantly.

design_tokens.colors: Frozen column bg: oklch(0.99 0.005 250). Sticky header bg: same. Border between frozen and scrolling: 1px oklch(0.85 0.005 250).
design_tokens.typography: Body: 13px tabular-nums. Header: 11px uppercase.
design_tokens.spacing: Row: 32px. Frozen column: 200px. Other columns: variable.
design_tokens.motion: None on scroll. Sort: instant.

component_architecture: <WideTable columns rows firstColumnFrozen />. Uses position: sticky on first <th> and first <td> per row, with z-index to stack above scrolling content.
interaction_details: Scroll right: first column stays frozen.
Scroll down: header stays sticky.
Sort header click: instant re-order.
Both frozen elements have z-index to layer correctly.
accessibility_considerations: Real <table> semantics. Sortable headers are buttons with aria-sort. Reduced-motion: no motion.
what_makes_this_distinctive: 1) CSS position: sticky on both first column AND header row — pure CSS, no JS scroll listeners. 2) z-index stacking ensures intersections (top-left cell) layer correctly. 3) Sort preserves frozen column behavior.

title: Tab management with Cmd+T new, Cmd+Shift+T reopen, Cmd+W close, drag-reorder
design_brief: You're building a browser-like tab system inside a multi-document editor (think Arc's tabs, VS Code's tabs, Figma's file tabs). Pain point: most in-app tab systems lack the depth of browser tab management.
anti_patterns_to_avoid: Tabs that wrap to second row (chaos)
ux_reasoning: Borrow ALL browser tab patterns: Cmd+T new, Cmd+W close, Cmd+Shift+T reopen, Cmd+1-9 jump to tab N, Cmd+] / Cmd+[ next/prev. Drag tabs to reorder. Closed tabs go to a recently-closed stack (size 10) that Cmd+Shift+T pops.

design_tokens.colors: Tab bg active: oklch(0.99 0.005 250). Tab bg inactive: oklch(0.93 0.005 250). Tab close X: oklch(0.55 0.005 250).
design_tokens.typography: Tab title: Inter 13px regular. Truncate at 160px.
design_tokens.spacing: Tab: 32px tall × variable width. Min 80px, max 200px.
design_tokens.motion: Drag reorder: 1:1 with FLIP layout on release.

component_architecture: <TabBar tabs activeId onSelect onClose onReorder>. useReopenClosedStack().
interaction_details: Cmd+T new tab, Cmd+W close, Cmd+Shift+T reopen last closed, Cmd+1-9 jump, Cmd+]/[ next/prev.
Drag tabs to reorder.
Closed-tab stack holds last 10 closed tabs for restoration.
accessibility_considerations: role='tablist' / role='tab' / aria-selected. Close buttons have aria-label. Reduced-motion: no animations.
what_makes_this_distinctive: 1) Full browser-tab keyboard model — Cmd+Shift+T reopens, Cmd+N jumps. 2) Closed-tab stack of 10 — undo for tab management. 3) Drag reorder respects keyboard model — no mouse required for anything.

title: Collaborative Kanban board with column ownership and live card animations
design_brief: You're building a Kanban board (think Trello, Linear cycles, Height projects). Multi-user drag, real-time updates, and column locks for moderation.
anti_patterns_to_avoid: Cards jumping when others reorder (you lose context)
No visual indicator who moved a card
No way to see who's currently dragging
ux_reasoning:
When a remote user drags a card, others see a soft ghost of the card following that user's pointer (in their color). When they release, the card animates to its final position with FLIP (200ms) and a brief author-color glow.

Columns can be 'pinned' by a moderator (no one else can reorder within them); attempted moves bounce back with brief shake.

design_tokens.colors: Card bg: white. Drag-by-other ghost: card content at 60% opacity in author color outline. Move-author glow: 200ms inner shadow in author color.
design_tokens.typography: Card title: Inter 13px medium. Description: 12px regular.
design_tokens.spacing: Card: variable height. Column gap: 12px.
design_tokens.motion: Remote move FLIP: 200ms cubic-bezier(0.22, 1, 0.36, 1). Author glow: 800ms fade.

component_architecture: <KanbanBoard cards columns onMove>. useRemoteDragGhosts() renders ghosts for other users' in-progress drags. FLIP on card moves.
interaction_details: Local drag: card follows pointer. Drop: FLIP animation places it.
Remote drag: ghost of card follows that user's pointer in their color.
After move: card briefly glows in author's color for 800ms.
Pinned columns prevent reorder; attempts bounce back.
accessibility_considerations: Each card is a real button. Drag has keyboard alternative (Cmd+Shift+arrow). Remote drag ghosts aria-hidden. Reduced-motion: FLIP becomes instant, glow becomes brief color border.
what_makes_this_distinctive: 1) Remote drag ghosts in author color — see who's about to move what before they commit. 2) Author-color glow after move — attribution that fades quickly. 3) Column lock with bounce-back — moderation without modal.

title: Notification toast stack with hover-to-pause and per-toast actions
design_brief: You're building the toast notification system (think Sonner, react-hot-toast, but better). Pain point: most toast libs auto-dismiss while user is reading or hovering; multiple stacked toasts compete for attention.
anti_patterns_to_avoid: Generic toast styles for success/error/info that all look the same
Stacking that hides earlier toasts behind new ones
ux_reasoning:
Toasts should be ambient and respectful. Auto-dismiss after 4s default; hovering ANY toast in the stack pauses ALL dismiss timers (don't punish the reader). Each toast can have one inline action ('Undo', 'Open').

Stack behavior: newest at top, fanned slightly so older ones are partially visible behind. Max 4 visible; older ones expire silently after 8s.

design_tokens.colors: Success: oklch(0.55 0.14 145) accent. Error: oklch(0.6 0.18 25). Info: oklch(0.55 0.18 250). Bg: oklch(0.18 0.005 250) text white.
design_tokens.typography: Toast title: Inter 13px medium. Description: 12px regular.
design_tokens.spacing: Toast: 320px wide × auto height. Stack: bottom-right, 8px gap.
design_tokens.motion: Enter: 200ms cubic-bezier(0.22, 1, 0.36, 1) slide + opacity. Dismiss: 150ms opacity. Hover pause: timer freeze.

component_architecture: <ToastProvider /> renders the stack. useToast() hook dispatches. Internal timer per toast paused on stack hover.
interaction_details: toast({...}) dispatches a notification. Stacks at bottom-right.
Auto-dismiss after 4s default. Hovering the stack pauses ALL timers.
Each toast can have one inline action button.
Errors use role='alert' (assertive), others use 'status' (polite).
accessibility_considerations: Each toast has proper role/aria-live by kind. Action buttons are real buttons. Reduced-motion: enter animation disabled. Color: kind supplemented by left-border thickness and aria-live urgency.
what_makes_this_distinctive: 1) Hover-anywhere-in-stack pauses ALL timers — respects readers. 2) Per-toast inline action (Undo, Open) — actionable, not just informational. 3) role/aria-live varies by kind — errors interrupt, info doesn't.

title: Stacked area chart with hover-to-isolate series and click-to-pin
design_brief: You're building a stacked area chart (think Datadog metrics, Grafana queries). Stacked areas show total + breakdown; users need to interrogate individual series.
anti_patterns_to_avoid: Stack that hides series shape (everything looks like the total)
No way to isolate a series
Click toggles visibility (loses the stacked view entirely)
ux_reasoning: Hovering a series in the stack: that series stays bright; others dim to 20%. Reveals its individual shape against the dimmed background. Hover off restores all. CLICK pins the isolation (others stay dim until clicked again or Esc).

design_tokens.colors: Stack: 6-color palette from OKLCH (same as funnel/dataviz palette). Dimmed: 0.2 opacity.
design_tokens.typography: Legend: 12px. Tooltip value: 13px tabular-nums.
design_tokens.spacing: Chart: 480×280. Legend: bottom horizontal.
design_tokens.motion: Series opacity transition: 150ms ease-out. Pin transition: 200ms ease-out.

component_architecture: <StackedAreaChart series data /> with hover + click state. Series rendered as <path> with opacity bound to hover/pin state.
interaction_details: Hover series: that series stays bright, others dim to 20%. Reveals shape.
Click series: pins isolation. Click again or Esc to unpin.
Legend buttons mirror chart hover/click behavior.
accessibility_considerations: Each series area has aria-label='series N: values'. Legend buttons are real buttons. Reduced-motion: opacity transition becomes instant.
what_makes_this_distinctive: 1) Hover-to-isolate reveals individual shapes hidden in the stack. 2) Click-to-pin keeps isolation through other interactions. 3) Legend and chart are bidirectionally bound.

title: First-run dataset import with sample data and 'replace later' upgrade path
design_brief: Building data-tool onboarding (think Airtable, Notion DBs, Retool). Pain point: empty app is intimidating; importing real data is friction.
anti_patterns_to_avoid: Empty-state with 'Import CSV' as only path
ux_reasoning: Pre-load 3 sample datasets that look real and let user explore. A small banner at top of any sample 'Replace with your data' deep-links into the proper import flow when user is ready. Sample status is a soft tag visible only in dataset settings.

design_tokens.colors: Replace banner: oklch(0.96 0.005 250) bg with 1px border. Text: oklch(0.4 0.005 250).
design_tokens.typography: Banner: 12px regular.
design_tokens.spacing: Banner: 32px tall above dataset.
design_tokens.motion: None.

component_architecture: <DatasetView dataset isSample showReplaceCTA>. <SampleBanner /> shown only when isSample.
interaction_details: Sample datasets pre-loaded; user can explore immediately.
Soft top banner offers replace path.
Banner disappears when dataset is replaced.
accessibility_considerations: Banner has role='status'. Replace is a real button.
what_makes_this_distinctive: 1) Sample data is realistic — user gets to see what a populated app looks like immediately. 2) Replace path is one click — never forced. 3) No 'sample' markers on individual rows — only on the dataset as a whole.

title: Phone number input with country detection and pretty formatting
design_brief: Building a phone input (think Stripe, Twilio, WhatsApp setup). Pain point: most phone inputs require manual country code selection and don't format as you type.
anti_patterns_to_avoid: Lose user input on country change
ux_reasoning: On focus, default to user's locale country. As user types digits, format per country's convention ('+1 555-123-4567' for US, '+44 20 7946 0958' for UK). If user pastes a number with explicit country code, auto-switch country.

design_tokens.colors: Input border: oklch(0.85 0.005 250) idle, oklch(0.55 0.18 250) focus, oklch(0.6 0.18 25) error.
design_tokens.typography: Input: 16px mono tabular. Country chip: 14px.
design_tokens.spacing: Input: 40px tall. Country chip: inline left of input.
design_tokens.motion: None on format. Country switch: instant.

component_architecture: <PhoneInput value onChange>. Internal country state. formatNumber(value, country) returns prettified string.
interaction_details: Type digits: formatted live per country convention.
Paste number with country code: auto-detect and switch country.
Country dropdown inline left — no separate field.
accessibility_considerations: Input has aria-label. Country select is native. Reduced-motion: no change.
what_makes_this_distinctive: 1) Format-as-you-type per country — looks like real phone numbers. 2) Country chip inline, not separate field — single visual unit. 3) Paste with country code auto-switches country — respects user shortcut.

title: 3D-rotated logo customizer with materials and lighting
design_brief: Building a logo preview tool (think Apple's Pages icon previewer, Sketch's vector → 3D plugin). User uploads SVG; sees it as a 3D object with material/lighting choices.
anti_patterns_to_avoid: No way to download a snapshot
ux_reasoning:
Convert SVG to extruded geometry (THREE.ExtrudeGeometry). Auto-rotate by default at slow speed; user can grab to spin. Material picker as horizontal strip below (metal, plastic, glass, paper) — each renders the same SVG with different material in <300ms.

Lighting via 3-point setup (key + fill + back). One slider for 'time of day' that adjusts key light angle.

design_tokens.colors: Bg: vertical gradient. Material thumbnails: 64px square renders.
design_tokens.typography: Material name: 11px uppercase tracking-wider.
design_tokens.spacing: Canvas: 60vh. Strip: 80px bottom.
design_tokens.motion: Auto-rotate: 0.005 rad/frame. Material swap: 300ms ease-out crossfade.

component_architecture: <LogoCustomizer svg>. <ExtrudedSvg /> uses ExtrudeGeometry from SVG path. <MaterialPicker materials onSelect />.
interaction_details: Default auto-rotate at slow speed.
Drag canvas: hijack rotation; release returns to auto-rotate after 2s.
Click material chip: 300ms crossfade to new material.
Snapshot download button takes current frame as PNG.
accessibility_considerations: Canvas has aria-label. Materials are real buttons. Reduced-motion: auto-rotate disabled.
what_makes_this_distinctive: 1) Auto-rotate by default — preview is alive without user input. 2) Material swap is fast (<300ms) — exploration is immediate. 3) SVG → extruded 3D — turns 2D brand into 3D mockup.

title: Settings screen with iOS-style stack on mobile and split-view on desktop
design_brief: Building app settings (think iOS Settings vs macOS System Preferences). Same data, two different paradigms.
anti_patterns_to_avoid: Desktop split that loses selection state
ux_reasoning:
Mobile: top-level list of categories. Tap pushes a new screen onto a stack (iOS-style). Back button returns. Breadcrumb at top.

Desktop: left rail with categories, right pane with selected category's settings. URL state preserves selection.

design_tokens.colors: Mobile bg: white. Desktop sidebar: oklch(0.97 0.005 250).
design_tokens.typography: Category: 16px. Setting label: 14px. Setting value: 13px tabular.
design_tokens.spacing: Mobile row: 44px. Desktop sidebar: 220px.
design_tokens.motion: Mobile push: 300ms cubic-bezier(0.32, 0.72, 0, 1) slide. Desktop: instant pane switch.

component_architecture: <Settings categories>. Mobile uses route-stack pattern; desktop uses two-pane layout.
interaction_details: Mobile: tap category to push detail. Back returns. iOS-style slide.
Desktop: left rail + right pane. URL state preserves.
Same data structure powers both.
accessibility_considerations: role='list' on mobile categories. Real buttons throughout. Reduced-motion: push slide becomes instant. Keyboard nav on desktop: Tab through rail; selection follows.
what_makes_this_distinctive: 1) Same data, two paradigms — iOS push on mobile vs Mac split on desktop. 2) Modality-detected, not breakpoint-detected. 3) Mobile back stack matches OS expectations exactly.

title: Accessible autocomplete combobox following ARIA 1.2 pattern
design_brief: Building an autocomplete combobox primitive used everywhere (think Github's mentions, Linear's command palette). Pain point: most autocomplete UIs fail screen reader announcements badly.
anti_patterns_to_avoid: Custom div soup with hover-only behavior
ux_reasoning: Follow WAI-ARIA 1.2 Combobox spec exactly: input has role='combobox' aria-expanded aria-controls aria-activedescendant. Listbox has role='listbox' with role='option' children. Arrow keys navigate; Enter selects; Tab also selects + moves; Esc closes.

design_tokens.colors: Selected: oklch(0.94 0.04 250). Bg: white.
design_tokens.typography: Input: 14px. Options: 13px.
design_tokens.spacing: Option: 32px row.
design_tokens.motion: Open: 80ms opacity.

component_architecture: <Combobox value onChange options getLabel>. Internal listbox open state. ARIA wired correctly.
interaction_details: Type to filter. Arrow keys navigate. Enter selects + closes.
Tab also selects + moves focus.
Esc closes without selecting.
aria-activedescendant tracks focused option.
accessibility_considerations: Full WAI-ARIA 1.2 Combobox conformance. Tested with VoiceOver, NVDA, JAWS.
what_makes_this_distinctive: 1) Exact ARIA 1.2 compliance — works in all major screen readers without polish. 2) Tab-selects matches browser autocomplete convention. 3) aria-activedescendant pattern keeps real focus on input, virtual focus on option.

title: Skeleton loader that matches real content shape exactly
design_brief: Building loading skeletons (think Facebook's content placeholders, LinkedIn's feed skeletons, Apple News). Pain point: most skeletons are generic gray boxes that don't match real content, causing layout shift on hydration.
anti_patterns_to_avoid: Skeleton boxes that bear no resemblance to real content
Different heights between skeleton and real content
ux_reasoning:
Skeletons should match real content's dimensions exactly. Each skeleton component is built from the same structural shell as its real counterpart, with content replaced by gray blocks of the right height + variable width.

Subtle 1.5s shimmer (NOT pulse). Hydration: skeleton blocks fade out + real content fades in over 100ms — no layout shift.

design_tokens.colors: Skeleton bg: oklch(0.93 0.005 250). Shimmer: linear-gradient with 30% alpha highlight traveling.
design_tokens.typography: Skeleton blocks use real line-heights so heights match.
design_tokens.spacing: Same as real content.
design_tokens.motion: Shimmer: 1500ms linear infinite. Hydration: 100ms opacity crossfade.

component_architecture: <Skeleton width height />. <CardSkeleton /> is built from same shell as <Card /> with Skeleton primitives replacing text.
interaction_details: Skeleton renders immediately on data fetch.
Shimmer is subtle (1.5s) — not pulsing.
On data arrival: skeleton fades out, real content fades in — 100ms.
Shape exactly matches real component — no layout shift.
accessibility_considerations: aria-hidden on decorative shimmer. Parent should have aria-busy='true' during loading. Reduced-motion: shimmer disabled.
what_makes_this_distinctive: 1) Skeleton component matches real card structure exactly — no layout shift on hydration. 2) Shimmer traveling-gradient, not pulse — subtle indication of activity. 3) 100ms hydration crossfade — perceptually instant.

title: Memphis design playlist UI with primary colors and geometric patterns
design_brief: Building a music player aesthetic in the spirit of Memphis Group (Ettore Sottsass, 1981) — primary colors, geometric patterns, jagged shapes. Pain point: most 'Memphis-inspired' UIs are pastiche with pastel colors instead of bold primaries.
anti_patterns_to_avoid: Smooth gradients (Memphis is hard color blocks)
ux_reasoning: Real Memphis: primary RGB + black, hard color edges, geometric pattern overlays (squiggles, triangles, dots), playful asymmetric layout that still functions.

design_tokens.colors: Red oklch(0.55 0.22 25), Blue oklch(0.5 0.22 250), Yellow oklch(0.85 0.18 90), Black oklch(0.10 0 0). NO gradient.
design_tokens.typography: Header: 'Arial Black' or display sans 32px. Body: 'Tahoma' 14px.
design_tokens.spacing: Intentional asymmetry — track items offset by random amounts within constraint.
design_tokens.motion: Hover: rotate 2deg + color invert.

component_architecture: <MemphisPlaylist tracks>. Background SVG patterns. Each track card has unique offset.
interaction_details: Asymmetric track layout (offset per row).
Hover: rotates 2deg counterclockwise.
Decorative SVG patterns in corners (geometric shapes).
No gradients, hard color blocks.
accessibility_considerations: Tracks are real buttons. Decorative SVG aria-hidden. Color contrast: white text on saturated colors meets 4.5:1. Reduced-motion: rotation disabled.
what_makes_this_distinctive: 1) Real Memphis primary palette (red/blue/yellow/black) — not pastel pastiche. 2) Intentional asymmetric offset per row. 3) Geometric SVG patterns as decoration — not background image, real vector.

title: Sortable people directory with last-active heatmap and inline skill chips
design_brief: Building company directory (think Linear's members, Notion's people, Slack's profiles). Pain point: most directories are avatars + name and miss the depth.
anti_patterns_to_avoid: Big avatar cards with no information
ux_reasoning: Dense table view: avatar (24px) + name + role + team + last-active heatmap (last 7 days as 7 cells) + skill chips. Sort by any column. Filter by skill chip click.

design_tokens.colors: Active cell green oklch(0.55 0.14 145). Inactive gray. Skill chip: oklch(0.94 0.04 250).
design_tokens.typography: Name: 13px medium. Role: 12px regular oklch(0.55).
design_tokens.spacing: Row: 40px. Heatmap cell: 8px square × 7.
design_tokens.motion: Skill chip add: 80ms opacity.

component_architecture: <PeopleDirectory people />. <Person /> row. <LastActiveHeatmap days />.
interaction_details: Each row: avatar + name + team + 7-day activity heatmap + skill chips.
Click skill chip: filter directory to people with that skill.
Multiple skill filters AND together.
Heatmap intensity scales with activity count.
accessibility_considerations: Real <table>. Skill chips are real buttons. Heatmap cells have aria-label='Mon: 3 events'. Reduced-motion: chip animations disabled.
what_makes_this_distinctive: 1) 7-day activity heatmap inline per person — micro-trend visible without click. 2) Skill chips ARE the filter — click to add to query. 3) Avatar + name + role in one column — density without crowding.

title: Hierarchical keyboard shortcut help overlay (press ?)
design_brief: Building shortcut discoverability (think Linear's help, Notion, GitHub). Pain point: most apps hide shortcuts in settings; users never learn.
anti_patterns_to_avoid: No way to copy a shortcut
ux_reasoning: Press ? to open a searchable shortcut overlay grouped by category (Navigation, Actions, Editing). Each shortcut shows keys as kbd-styled chips. Search filters across all categories.

design_tokens.colors: Overlay bg: oklch(0.18 0.005 250 / 0.85) backdrop-blur. Modal: white. kbd chip: oklch(0.95 0.005 250) bg with 1px border.
design_tokens.typography: Category: 14px medium. Shortcut label: 13px. kbd: 'JetBrains Mono' 11px.
design_tokens.spacing: Modal: 640px wide centered.
design_tokens.motion: Open: 150ms ease-out opacity.

component_architecture: <ShortcutHelp shortcuts groupBy>. Hook listens for '?' globally.
interaction_details: Press ? anywhere: opens shortcut overlay.
Search filters across all categories.
Each shortcut shows kbd-styled key chips.
Esc closes.
accessibility_considerations: role='dialog' with focus trap. Search input is the autofocused first focusable. kbd elements are semantic. Reduced-motion: open animation disabled.
what_makes_this_distinctive: 1) ? as the universal shortcut to see shortcuts — Linear/Notion convention. 2) Searchable across all shortcuts — discover unknown keys. 3) Real kbd elements with styled chips — semantic + visual.

title: Live cursor chat with ephemeral text bubbles
design_brief: Building a 'cursor chat' feature for whiteboards/canvases (think Figma's quick chat, tldraw's cursor messages). Press / to start typing a message that appears next to your cursor for others to see.
ux_reasoning: Press / on canvas: input appears next to your cursor. Type message; press Enter to broadcast (or Esc to cancel). Message appears as floating bubble next to YOUR cursor for OTHER users for 8 seconds, then fades. Quick, ephemeral, non-disruptive.

design_tokens.colors: Bubble bg: oklch(0.18 0.005 250) text white. Per-user color tint on border 2px.
design_tokens.typography: Bubble text: 13px.
design_tokens.spacing: Bubble: 12px padding, anchored 16px from cursor.
design_tokens.motion: Bubble appear: 150ms ease-out. Fade out: 300ms after 8s.

component_architecture: <CursorChat onSend>. Hook handles / keypress.
interaction_details: Press /: input appears next to cursor.
Type message, Enter to send. Esc cancels.
Other users see your bubble in your color for 8s.
Bubble fades over 300ms after timeout.
accessibility_considerations: Input has aria-label. Remote bubbles aria-live='polite' to announce messages. Reduced-motion: appear animation disabled.
what_makes_this_distinctive: 1) / shortcut anywhere on canvas opens chat — keyboard-first. 2) Ephemeral 8s lifetime — never clutters. 3) Bubble next to cursor — context preserved.

title: Form submit with state-aware button morph (loading → success → idle)
design_brief: Building a submit button used everywhere (think Stripe payment, login forms). Pain point: most submit buttons have a separate spinner OR a layout-shifting label change.
anti_patterns_to_avoid: Label that jumps width on state change
ux_reasoning: Button has 3 states: idle, loading, success. Same width on all states (longest label sets the width). Icon morphs: from arrow → spinner → checkmark. After success, returns to idle after 1500ms.

design_tokens.colors: Idle: brand color. Loading: same. Success: green. Text: white.
design_tokens.typography: 14px medium.
design_tokens.spacing: 40px tall, width fits longest state label.
design_tokens.motion: State morph: 200ms cubic-bezier(0.22, 1, 0.36, 1). Icon swap: 100ms opacity.

component_architecture: <SubmitButton state label>. State is 'idle' | 'loading' | 'success'.
interaction_details: Click: state → loading. Icon morphs to spinner.
On success: state → success (green bg + check). Returns to idle after 1500ms.
Min-width prevents layout shift between states.
accessibility_considerations: aria-busy='true' during loading. Disabled state during non-idle. Reduced-motion: spin disabled.
what_makes_this_distinctive: 1) Same width across all states — no layout shift. 2) Icon morphs (arrow → spinner → check) — visual story of state. 3) Auto-return to idle — no manual reset needed.

title: Donut chart with hover-explode segment and center-readout swap
design_brief: Building a donut chart for budget/category breakdowns (think Mint, YNAB, Apple Health activity). Pain point: most donuts have a static center label and weak hover.
anti_patterns_to_avoid: Legend takes more space than chart
Center label doesn't update with hover
ux_reasoning: Donut segments translate outward 4px on hover. The CENTER label updates to show the hovered segment's value + percentage of total. Mouse leaves: returns to total in center.

design_tokens.colors: Segments: 6-color OKLCH palette. Hover translate: 4px outward.
design_tokens.typography: Center: 32px tabular-nums. Center label: 12px uppercase.
design_tokens.spacing: Donut: 240px outer, 140px inner. Legend: vertical right side.
design_tokens.motion: Segment translate: 200ms ease-out. Center text swap: 80ms opacity.

component_architecture: <DonutChart segments />. SVG with arcs. Hover state for segment.
interaction_details: Hover segment: translates outward 4px; center label updates to that segment.
Mouse leave: returns to total.
Click segment: could pin (extension).
accessibility_considerations: Each segment is path with aria-label. Underlying data available in sr-only list. Reduced-motion: translate disabled.
what_makes_this_distinctive: 1) Center label is LIVE — shows hovered segment value, not just total. 2) Segment translates outward on hover — physical feedback. 3) Single source of truth — no separate tooltip competing with center.

title: Empty state with composer pre-focused and three example prompts
design_brief: Building a new-doc empty state for an AI chat / blank canvas tool (think ChatGPT new chat, Cursor blank file, Notion blank page). Pain point: empty states with sad illustrations or 'Get started' buttons.
ux_reasoning: Composer at center pre-focused. Below: 3 example prompts as ghost buttons. Click any to populate composer. No tour, no welcome — start typing immediately.

design_tokens.colors: Composer border: oklch(0.85 0.005 250). Example bg: oklch(0.97 0.005 250) on hover.
design_tokens.typography: Composer: 16px regular. Example: 13px regular italic.
design_tokens.spacing: Composer: max-w 640px centered.
design_tokens.motion: None.

component_architecture: <EmptyState examples onSubmit>.
interaction_details: Composer pre-focused on mount.
3 example prompts as italic buttons; click to populate composer.
Enter submits; Shift+Enter newline.
No CTAs, no welcome.
accessibility_considerations: Textarea has placeholder. Examples are real buttons. Reduced-motion: no change.
what_makes_this_distinctive: 1) Composer focused on mount — typing happens immediately. 2) Examples as inline italic prompts — invite without prescribe. 3) No illustration, no welcome — minimum chrome, maximum agency.
